predicates.description: who holds the object at this scene (one holder at a time)
the load-bearing wreck-night truth a subject is the cause of
where a load-bearing object stands, as a state that changes when it is found or moved
the state of a light — burning, out, or shown to have been doused by a hand

facts.claim: On the wreck-night the tide-bell did not ring the turn of the tide.
On the wreck-night the Holm light stood dark across the Sneck where it should have burned.
Halsa woke in the gale, found the tide-bell silent and the Holm light dark, and said the light was out.
Halsa had never once in her life seen the Holm light fail and could not say why it had failed.
Orne Veck stood in the lee of the chapel wall on the wreck-night, watching the Sneck, with a thing hidden under his coat against his ribs.
Orne knew the Holm light was dark and knew the hour, and was set that no one on the Holm must learn the light had been made dark.
Orne had carried the lantern to the Ness that night and shown a light there, then stood in the chapel's lee and let the sea do the rest as a hull ground onto the Sneck.

object.value: false light shown on the Ness

facts.claim: On the wreck-night the lantern was carried by Orne out to the Ness.
Bryde saw the Holm light go out from below and climbed the light-stair in the dark to relight it.
On the wet fourth turn of the stair Bryde's foot went, Bryde fell hard against the stone and broke the leg under it, and lay unable to climb or call while the light stayed dark.
By grey morning the wind had dropped and the shore was a ruin of broken ship timber; the Sneck had wrecked a vessel in the night.
The wrecked vessel was the Marran.
The Drang, the Holm's one shingle road to the mainland at low water, was drowned under the high tide, shutting the Holm in until the long ebb.
Eda said the Sneck is a wicked piece of water, that it was honest weather and a black night with no light to steer by, and that there would be men drowned under the timber.
The officer noted that no soul could leave the Holm until the ebb bared the road, so whatever there was to find was trapped on the island.
Bryde lay abed in the keeper's room with the broken leg splinted between two oar-blades.
Bryde told Halsa she would keep the tally while Bryde was down, since someone must keep the Holm's account.
Halsa took the keeper's tally-book, a long ledger logging every tide, lamp-filling and barrel landed in Bryde's hand.
The tally's last line, in Bryde's own hand, read 'Light out' with an hour beside it.
Halsa saw that the hour the light went out was the same hour the Marran struck the bar.
Bryde would not say how the light came to be out, but said only 'I did not douse it', to be written in the tally.
Wick came up from the shore at a run, white-faced, and told it first a drowned man on the bar, then a cask, then only a lantern, saying he did not know which it was.
Senna said Wick was lying, that he had found a real thing and hidden it in the loft under the straw, and was too frightened of it to keep one telling.
Halsa marked that under the boy's three tellings was one real found thing and a fright, but did not yet know what.
The officer set up a Crown count above the tide-line and tallied every cask, bale and crate the sea brought up off the Marran, taking the islanders' word written.
The officer did not yet know his count was short and had no way to know; he wrote in good faith the numbers the islanders gave him.
Pell Garrow said his master was owed bonded brandy off the Marran and that he held the lading-bill saying how much, and would have his master's to the cask.
A living half-drowned man was pulled from the surf at the morning tide and carried to the curing-shed; he gave his name as Cass Pellow, mate of the Marran.
Cass said he was the only living soul off the Marran; that they struck in the black with no light where the light should be, she broke her back, and he was in the water.
Cass said he had hold of the supercargo a while, had his coat in his fist, and the sea took him out of his hand into the dark and under, and nothing could be done.
Halsa believed the mate's account that morning, took it for an honest loss to a black night and a bad bar, and was sorry for him.
The other survivor was a woman passenger, brought off living with a sea-chest she would not let go of and slept across, kept in the corner of the headman's house.
She gave her name as Ysolt Marran, of the Marran, said the chest was hers and locked, and that the mate must not have it whatever he claimed.

Ysolt knew, but did not say, that the drowned supercargo was her own kin, that he had carried the chest's key on his body, and that he carried a paper that stood against her and would unmake her claim.
Dunna Quick, first down at the ebb before any count, had taken one cask of brandy and hidden it behind her salt-sacks, calling it a plain gift of the sea that the Holm keeps.
Dunna said the headman himself had bid them, the night the timber came in, take what the sea gives and say nothing to the Crown's man of what came up before he was counting.
It never crossed Dunna's mind that anything but weather had brought the Marran to the bar; she thought the wreck a plain gift of the sea.
Maon Skerry read the wreck and said the heavy keel-timber had come up off the Ness end, whereas a ship lost honest on the Sneck in that wind always fetches up down-strand.
Maon said this ship was steering — she had laid her head for a light out on the Ness, where no light, mark, or leading-light belongs, and she steered to it.
Maon said he can still read what a light on the Ness would do to a vessel in this channel on a black night: it would draw her right onto the Sneck.
Maon said the Crown's iron buoys had taken his pilot's living from him years ago, but he never lost the knowing of the channel.
Maon would not name a hand, saying only that a light was shown that should not have been, and the rest was not his to say.
Maon had himself shown ships off the bar in the old days, in the old way.
Halsa heard that a ship had steered to a wrong light, but did not yet know whose.
Orne called the Holm together in the chapel and urged them to give the officer a plain account — a ship lost to the dark, a man drowned — and to keep the Holm's own counsel as always.
Orne said a wreck on the Sneck is an old sorrow taken by weather and a bad bar, and that what is the Holm's by old right is the Holm's, the Crown needing not the whole of their business.
Orne believed as he spoke that the boy had found only a drowned body, and did not know Wick had taken up off the bar the very lantern Orne carried to the Ness and hidden it in the straw.
Halsa watched the headman steer the whole Holm toward one telling and asked herself for the first time why a man so careful would do so over a wreck the sea brought in honest.
Pell Garrow said the count was short against his lading-bill by one cask of brandy that was aboard but had not come ashore.
Pell said the sea does not drink brandy and a cask does not walk, so someone lifted it — an islander at the ebb, or else the surviving mate, whom he would look at hardest.

Cass heard the suspicion fall on the islanders and was content to let it lie, since the wrangle over a cask kept every eye off the dead man's lading-bill he kept hidden and off the dead man's claim he meant to make his own.
Cass kept the dead supercargo's lading-bill hidden against his own body.

Bryde said the light was already out when the climb up the stair began — gone all at once like a hand on it, not the slow guttering of a failing lamp on the full reservoir Bryde had filled at sundown.
Bryde said a hand doused that light and not the weather, but said he never saw the hand, being down off the stair before reaching the lamp, and could not tell whose hand it was.
Halsa now believed the dark light was no accident and that the wreck on the bar and the doused light over it were one matter, not two.
Wick told Halsa the true telling at last: a lantern, up high on the bar, dry as a bone and shuttered, no man — which he hid under the straw because he was afraid.
Senna pointed out where the lantern was hidden, under the straw by the third post, and said Wick would not have hidden it had he not known it a wrong thing to find.
Wick had hidden the lantern under the straw by the third post of the net-loft.

object.value: hidden under the straw in the net-loft

facts.claim: Halsa dug the lantern from the straw and recognized it as Holm-work, local make, not ship's gear.

Halsa saw the lantern was dry through, its wick crusted with burnt oil and its reservoir near empty — it had been lit, burned long till the oil was near gone, then shuttered and left high on the bar where no wave touched it.

Halsa pieced together that someone showed a false light off the Ness to draw the Marran onto the bar, doused the true light so she'd not be saved, and the starving Holm took what the lie washed up — one purpose, one hand — though she did not yet know the hand.
The ebb was making: the grey water drew off the Drang, the first black spine of the shingle road showed, and the topsail of the Crown cutter stood in toward the Sneck with the falling tide.
The supercargo had not simply drowned; a hand held him under in the surf to take what he carried — hidden still, deeper than the rest.

object.value: held the supercargo under to drown him for the lading-bill

facts.claim: Halsa knew the Marran had been made to wreck and the Holm had drunk of it, but did not yet know and had no way to know the supercargo had been held under rather than simply drowned.

Halsa weighed three roads: carry it all to the officer and let the Crown's law come down on the Holm; bury it and give the officer the plain wreck the headman wanted; or call the Holm itself down to name its own wrong before the law could.

Orne too watched the Drang bare from the chapel wall, reckoning the tide; he meant to get the lantern back before the Crown could lay hands on it and the cargo away, the whole made thing buried under the face of an honest wreck.
Halsa carried the lantern and the keeper's tally to the officer and set them on his Crown ledger, choosing to bring it to the Crown.

Halsa told the officer the lantern was Holm-work not off the ship, found dry high on the bar where no sea reached, carried and burned there, and that the tally's 'Light out' hour was the hour the Marran struck.
Halsa said she would say the wreck was made — a light put out by a hand, a light shown where none belongs — but would not name by whom, not being sure and not willing to fit a name to a rope.

The officer said he could have written it a mislaid lantern and a lamp failed in a blow, but understood Halsa had handed him a made wreck — not a short cask or a fine — and that once he opened it on the trapped island there was no half of it.
Halsa said she would not be the Holm's law, only not its lie, and that what the Crown did with it was the Crown's not hers.
The officer drew the clean page to him, wrote at its head, and began the inquiry.

The officer began with the chest, took it into Crown keeping over Ysolt's protest, and when she would not give the key — saying it went down with the man who carried it — had it broken open with a bar on the headman's table.

The chest held no coin, plate or treasure but papers: a contested deed with inheritance papers under it that Ysolt sailed to lodge ashore, and a second deed standing against it, which the officer guessed the drowned man carried.
The officer told Ysolt that a man who carried the one paper that could break her claim was worth a great deal more to someone dead than alive.

Ysolt's composure broke and she said the drowned man was her own cousin, who carried the key and the filthy deed they had got up against her and was bringing it ashore to ruin her, and now it was all the Crown's.
Halsa, hearing 'worth more dead than alive', let herself think plainly for the first time that the supercargo's death might have been no honest drowning.

Before the officer, with the lantern on the table, Maon swore a light on the Ness draws a ship onto the Sneck, that this was done to the Marran — but that he did not light it, his hands clean of the Marran, though he owned he had shown ships off the bar in the bad old days.

The officer matched the lantern's make and burning to a shore-light, Holm-work, carried to the headland and shown, and declared the false light named to the Crown, the question left being whose hand carried it.

Orne, with the very lantern not a foot from his hand, said he knew nothing of any light on the Ness and laid the Holm's dark light to weather — an old keeper, a wet stair, a wild night, a lamp that can fail — and would not own one grain of it.
Orne had carried that lantern to the Ness with his own hands, shown the false light, and doused the true, and faced with the proof on the table would not own it.

Dunna, questioned and trembling, gave up that she had taken the one cask of brandy at the ebb before any count, but only because the headman bid them take what the sea gives; she never lit nor knew of any light and would give the cask back rather than hang for his wreck.
Dunna gave up the short cask to the Crown.

Pell, seeing his short cask come into the count, said then it was not the mate but the island that lifted it, the headman's word behind it, and that he would have hanged the mate for it; he was leaving with a hanging where he came for brandy.
The mate ran for the bared Drang at the ebb toward the mainland and the cutter, the dead supercargo's lading-bill buttoned inside his shirt.
Wick saw the mate running the Drang and cried out for the Holm to stop him, bringing the island down onto the shore to head him off.
The mate faltered on the narrow road with water either side and was taken by the Holm on the shingle.

Halsa drew the sea-stained lading-bill from inside the mate's shirt and knew the last of it: he had not lost the supercargo to the sea but taken the bill off the man's body, having drowned him by a hand in the surf for what he carried.
Halsa said to the mate, 'You held him under', not as a question, and the mate's not-saying was enough.
Senna held Wick back at the loft door from the catching, telling him his part — finding the lantern and telling it true — was done, and the two children held the loft while the Holm took the mate.

The Crown cutter came over the bared Drang on the dead low water and the officer gave the mate up first for the supercargo's death and the stolen bill, then the headman for the false light and the made wreck.
Halsa watched them take Orne Veck down to the cutter's boat and did not look away, saying it was the law's now, not hers — she only would not be its lie — though her eyes stung.

Maon, cleared and at the water's edge, offered to pilot the Crown's cutter over the Sneck, saying he was clean of the Marran by his own word and believed, and that he had held his tongue too long once and would not do so twice.
Eda, salting spoiled fish, said she had thought it the weather all week, honest weather as ever on the Sneck, and it was Orne — Orne who married her own cousin, whom she had known forty years.

Bryde, hearing the cutter's gun, said the doused light was owned to the Crown — not buried, not laid on Bryde, not gone into the dark — and was glad of it whatever it cost, bidding the keeper write in the tally 'The light was owned'.

Ysolt left on the cutter with her chest broke but her deed lodged safe in Crown hands and her claim sound and her kin's death named, saying her cousin had come to ruin her with the deed and she'd have hated him living but no one deserved to be held under in the surf for a paper.
Pell went to the boat with his master's cargo restored to the Crown's tally and the count made whole, the short cask back, saying he came for brandy and watched a man hang himself with his own light.

Dunna stood apart as the Holm turned from her, having given the cask back and told the officer true and kept her neck, but knowing she was the one who named Orne to the Crown and would be marked for it — a neck and no name.

Halsa filled the reservoir at sundown and set the great lamp burning steady and yellow over the Sneck — the true light over the channel where the false one had drawn the Marran in — and the tide-bell rang the turn that evening as it always had.
Halsa told Wick that all she had done was make the Holm stop lying.

Halsa let the wreck stand as the sea's: she came alone with the lantern wrapped in sacking meant for the officer, looked at the shut starving island and the cutter standing in, thought of the rope and whose necks, and could not do it.
Halsa went to the officer's table with only the keeper's tally, open to a line she had copied fresh and plain: 'Light failed in the gale. Keeper injured climbing to relight.'

Halsa told the officer the light failed in the gale, that Bryde went up to relight it and fell and broke a leg, the Marran struck in the dark of it, and it was the Sneck's hard chance — she keeps the tally and that's the tally.
The officer, never shown the dry lantern and not knowing the count short, took the islander's straight word and wrote it a loss to the weather and the Sneck, asking nothing further.

Bryde answered with grief, not gladness, saying 'Failed' and that the keeper knew what was climbed for, but would keep the bed and keep the mouth if that was the road Halsa chose — a heavy thing, heavier than the leg.

Orne came to Halsa at dusk and held out his hand for the boy's lantern off the bar, saying she had done the hard part keeping the Holm off the rope and should not leave the thing lying for the next Crown man — give it him and the Holm is safe for good.
Halsa weighed one drowned stranger's truth against the whole life of the island, knew she had already chosen, and gave the lantern to Orne.

That night Orne lifted a flag of the salt-house floor, dug into the cold sand beneath, laid the lantern in, and trod the floor flat over it — the false light buried where the sea had failed to bury it.

Maon came on Orne in the salt-house dark with the spade and said only that buried is not undone — it is only put where it cannot be answered for — then went out and said no more of it to any living soul.

Ysolt came to the headman before the cutter and made a bargain: the Crown having written it weather means no inquiry and no one breaks open her chest for evidence; she goes ashore with her chest unopened and her affairs her own, and in return she never carried a chest off the island and never saw a wreck that was anything but a wreck.
Orne agreed to the bargain, knowing the worth of a kept mouth.

Halsa, hearing of the bargain after, let it stand — let the passenger keep her chest closed and the deed unread, and the supercargo's death stay an honest drowning forever — and never knew, now, what a hand had truly done in the surf.

On this road Cass kept the dead supercargo's lading-bill and the dead man's claim to the cargo as his own; his hand was never named, and he walked off the Sneck a free survivor, mourned for his ordeal, having drowned a man for a paper.

Wick could not carry the knowing and went down white and shaking to the officer's door to tell that he found a dry lantern on the bar, that the light was put out by a hand, and the ship was made to wreck.

Senna caught Wick at the officer's door and hauled him back, telling him that telling it true there would hang half the island — Dunna, Orne, themselves — and himself with it; it would not be brave, only loud.

Halsa came on them and took the boy by the shoulders, said Senna was right and the choice was hers to carry not his, that he had put it on the children, and gave him the telling to hold: he found a ship's lantern washed up, took it for kindling, and it's gone.
Dunna kept the short cask hid behind her salt-sacks and her mouth shut, and the count was never made right to the factor, the wreck being weather and no one counting twice.

The cutter came over the bared Drang and the officer carried off a plain wreck's report and nothing more, telling the master a vessel was lost on the Sneck in a black gale, her light failed her, the keeper laid up, one mate and one passenger living, the rest drowned.
The officer suspected nothing of any false light, of the Ness, or of a hand in the dark; he had been given a clean grey wreck and believed it.

Pell, unsatisfied and able to prove nothing, told the officer his count was short by one cask and he'd lay his life an islander had it up the rocks, but it was written weather with no inquiry, so he'd go home short and tell his master the sea drank it.
Halsa watched the cutter take the truth away unseen, the Holm whole behind her and shut and safe, the light true again on its stair, the island set to last — and she was no longer clean of any of it.
The cutter gone and the Drang drowned again, the Holm gathered at the curing-shed fire the first shut evening as it gathered after any hard sea.
Orne sat in the headman's place, which no one had taken from him, but sat quiet and did not speak for the room as he once would have, and the room felt the difference.
Dunna talked to fill the silence, saying it a mercy the road was shut, that the count came right and the factor went content with no harm to a soul, but no one took it up and she went quiet.

Eda, knowing the least and so the only one easy, said it a hard week with poor souls under the timber but not one of theirs among them, only strangers drowned, the Holm whole — a light loss as the Sneck goes.
Ysolt sat apart, held her cup and did not drink, her eyes going once to the fire when Eda spoke of strangers drowned, saying nothing — she had made a bargain of saying nothing and kept it.

Bryde, carried down with the bad leg out on a stool, watched the headman in his place and kept the true account behind a still face, as it would be kept now, written and hidden against a day that might never come.
Senna told Wick the knowing gets smaller the longer you don't say it, like a stone in the sea that stays but wears.
Wick said low to Senna that he keeps feared he'll forget which telling is the true one, and then thinks maybe that is the mending of it — when he can't mind anymore which one was true.
Over the gathering the great lamp burned true and yellow over the Sneck and the tide-bell rang the turn as it always had, and the Holm — whole, shut, and lying still — went on with its silence kept.
Halsa called the whole Holm to the bared shingle at the ebb, going hut to hut with the lantern wrapped under her arm, and the whole island came down — including last and unhurried the headman.

Halsa told the gathered Holm she would not carry it to the Crown behind their backs nor bury it under a salt-house floor; whatever was done the wreck-night was done by and to the Holm, and the Holm would hear it named by themselves before the law came and named it for them.

Orne came down to the front to put the gathering down, to say the girl was overwrought and it was weather and to go home, not knowing the thing under her arm was the very lantern he carried to the Ness — he thought it talk.

Maon climbed onto the high shoulder of the bar and told the gathered Holm that a light on the Ness draws a ship onto the Sneck, that the Marran steered to a light on the Ness — read in the lie of her timber — and a light was shown out there the wreck-night.

Halsa held up the dry Holm-work lantern before the whole Holm and named it the false light — not off any ship, burned dry, carried out, left high on the bar — shown from the Ness to bring the Marran onto the Sneck, from the island, by one of them.

Wick stepped out and said before the Holm that he found the lantern on the bar at first light, dry and shuttered, hid it in the loft straw out of fear, told it a man and a cask, and would not say it any way but true again — Senna at his shoulder.
Orne came down to take it in hand, his eye fell on the lantern, the chapel voice failed him, and he said 'Aye, it's mine. I'll not stand here and let the boy carry it. It's mine.'

Orne confessed before the Holm: they were starving, the spring and autumn catches failed; knowing what a light on the Ness does, he carried the lantern out, lit it on the Ness, doused their own so the ship would not be answered, and came back to the chapel wall and let the sea do the rest.

object.value: showed the false light on the Ness and doused the Holm light to wreck the Marran

facts.claim: Orne said he told himself it was for the island and that he never laid a hand on a man, only on a lamp, but that doing it for them did not make it less the thing it was — the light was his, the Marran his, do with him what they would.
Ysolt came forward with her chest, set it on the shingle, took a second key cut against the loss of the first from a cord at her throat, and opened it before the Holm.

object.value: opened on the bar before the Holm

facts.claim: Ysolt drew out the papers — a contested deed with her inheritance under it that she sailed to lodge ashore, and one against it that would break her claim — and said the man who carried the deed against her, who carried the key, was her own kin, the supercargo the mate said the sea took.

Ysolt challenged the mate: where is the key, carried on a cord like hers — the sea takes a man but does not unknot a cord and leave you the key — and where is the lading-bill; a man does not get a paper off a drowning kinsman's coat by grieving him.

Halsa, seeing the bottom of it, told the mate before the Holm: 'You held him under — in the surf, in the dark, for the bill, for what it was worth to you. That's why you alone came off her living. You made sure of it.'

Cass broke and confessed: the supercargo would have had it all, leaving him the only living officer with nothing; when they were both in the black water he had hold of him, did not let go to save him but held on the other way till he went quiet, took the bill off him, let the sea have the rest, and told them he slipped his grip — he drowned him for the paper.

object.value: held the supercargo under to drown him for the lading-bill

facts.claim: Pell, hearing the mate own a killing, said he came onto the rock for one short cask of brandy and would have hanged the man for that, and that his short cask was the least wrong done on this shore.

Dunna fetched the short cask down from her salt-house and set it among the recovered casks before the factor and all, owning she took it first down at the ebb on the headman's word, thinking it the sea's plain gift, never knowing of any light — the count whole by her own hand.

Halsa held the reckoning, saying the headman's wreck and the mate's killing were named on the Holm's own shore by the Holm's own people before the Crown set foot on the bar — whatever the law did now, it did to men the island had already judged.

Maon stood cleared, the false light owned to another hand, and said before them all that forty years he had held his tongue about the old trade and shown ships off the bar himself in the bad days, but it was named now and not his hand this time — his hands clean of the Marran, the silence done.

Bryde, carried down on a hurdle, told the Holm that the climb up the stair was to relight what the headman's hand put out, that the light was dark at the start and the stair wet, and the fall on the fourth turn broke the leg — so the doused light did not only drown a ship, it broke the keeper.

The officer came down last, found the inquiry already held by its own subjects on the bar, and said the Holm had judged its own before he could — the headman owning a made wreck, the mate a killing, a count made whole by the hand that broke it.

The officer said he would take the two of them for the cutter and the truth would go with them, but the truth was told here first by Halsa and he would set that down — the island named it, and that was not nothing.

Halsa met the cutter at the tide-line with the officer and gave up the mate for the supercargo's death and the headman for the made wreck, the telling already told by the island's own and not wrung out by the Crown.

Ysolt went to the boat with her deed shown and chest open, her claim spoken before witnesses and her kin's death named, telling Halsa that on the mainland they'd have wrung it out over months and called it justice, but the Holm did it in a morning on a beach — she'd come thinking she'd landed among thieves and was wrong.

Pell went to the boat with his count whole, saying it was made whole by the very woman who lifted the cask, and a wrecker and murderer were owned to his face before he could lay a wrong charge — he came for brandy and leaves with a respect he didn't think to find.

Eda, among her spoiled fish, did not this time call it the sea's honest work, saying she had salted fish through a dozen wrecks and called every one weather and would have called this one the same — and it was Orne, whom she'd known forty years; she'd never again call a wreck on the bar the sea's plain work without thinking of this morning.

Senna, keeping the bar with Wick as the cutter pulled away, answered his question whether what they did was right by saying it was true, which is better than right — right you can argue, true you can't.

Wick, who had told it three ways, stood at the high shoulder of the bar where he first found the lantern and did not hide his face this time, having told it true at the last before the whole Holm, the stone out of him.

Halsa watched the cutter take the headman and the mate away, the island behind her whole — shut, shamed, grieving its headman, but owned and not lying — having kept the Holm together by making it stand on its own shore and name what it had done before the law could.
The light burned true that evening on its stair, the tide-bell rang the turn, and the Holm lived on, having judged its own before the law could, on the bar at the ebb.
